item.title: Deloitte and PwC tapped to monitor projects, agencies
item.description: THE National Monitoring and Coordination Authority (NMCA) will engage international firms Deloitte and PricewaterhouseCoopers (PwC) to support the monitoring, audits and review of government projects and agencies, says Chief Secretary Ivan Pomaleu. Pomaleu said one of the main reasons for establishing the NMCA was to improve the quality, consistency and reliability of national implementation data,
item.content:encoded:
THE National Monitoring and Coordination Authority (NMCA) will engage international firms Deloitte and PricewaterhouseCoopers (PwC) to support the monitoring, audits and review of government projects and agencies, says Chief Secretary Ivan Pomaleu.

Pomaleu said one of the main reasons for establishing the NMCA was to improve the quality, consistency and reliability of national implementation data, particularly from provinces and districts.

He said that the Government planned to progressively integrate monitoring systems, reporting templates, dashboards and digital platforms across agencies to allow more standardised reporting and real-time visibility over project implementation.

“The Government also intends to strengthen digital monitoring systems, reporting standards, audit visibility and cross-agency transparency mechanisms to support accountability,” he said.

“The objective is to move progressively toward evidence-based decision-making supported by more credible, timely and transparent implementation data.”

Pomaleu said the NMCA would operate within the Government’s existing legal, financial and administrative accountability frameworks.

The authority will be governed by a board comprising senior public officials and outside representatives, including the chief secretary as chairman, the secretaries of treasury, planning, and provincial and local level government affairs, the chairman and chief executive officer of the National Economic and Fiscal Commission, the director of the National Research Institute, and representatives from the business community, civil society and Transparency International

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: New Ireland’s shark-calling festival set for July
item.description: THE New Ireland government will hold the 2026 Shark-Calling Festival from July 16-18 at Kono village in West Coast Barok, Namatanai, to bring people together, says Governor Byron Chan. Backed by a provincial commitment of K100,000, the event is recognised on the national calendar and expected to lure national and international visitors.
item.content:encoded:
THE New Ireland government will hold the 2026 Shark-Calling Festival from July 16-18 at Kono village in West Coast Barok, Namatanai, to bring people together, says Governor Byron Chan.

Backed by a provincial commitment of K100,000, the event is recognised on the national calendar and expected to lure national and international visitors.

Chan said that the festival was not just a cultural showcase, but also an unifying event.

“The divisions that has been left among us whether through social distance or missed opportunities for partnership ends here,” he said.

“The West Coast Barok area will serve as the foundation upon which we rebuild our provincial friendship and cooperation.”

The shark-calling festival, rooted in the sacred traditions of the West Coast is regarded as a symbol of resilience and identity in New Ireland.

“We are using this festival to signal a return to one province, one people,” Chan said.

The sound of coconut rattles in Barok will be a call for New Irelanders to stand together under the leadership of Chan and his vision to heal divisions and create a unbreakable bond using the strength of the province’s diverse traditions.

The provincial events committee is extending an invitation to leaders, elders and families from across the province to attend.

The festival aims to revive partnerships, foster pride and showcase New Ireland’s traditions.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Marape welcomes dismissal of challenge
item.description: PRIME Minister James Marape has described the Supreme Court’s dismissal of a constitutional challenge as a landmark affirmation of the country’s constitutional order, parliamentary democracy, and political stability. “The court has now made it absolutely clear that Section 145(5) is constitutional and consistent with the spirit of responsible government envisioned by our Constitution,” Marape said
item.content:encoded:
PRIME Minister James Marape has described the Supreme Court’s dismissal of a constitutional challenge as a landmark affirmation of the country’s constitutional order, parliamentary democracy, and political stability.

“The court has now made it absolutely clear that Section 145(5) is constitutional and consistent with the spirit of responsible government envisioned by our Constitution,” Marape said.

“This decision is not merely about one political case or one prime minister.

“James Marape will not be here forever. This amendment is for the long-term stability of Papua New Guinea and for future governments that will continue to benefit from this law, just as we continue to benefit from good laws passed before us.”

On March 4, Opposition Leader James Nomane filed an application in the Supreme Court seeking a declaration that a Constitutional Amendment 48 (motion of no confidence) law 2025 was invalid for various reasons.

The Supreme Court, comprising justices David Cannings, George Manuhu, Derek Hartshorn, Joseph Yagi and Colin Makail, ruled that many of the issues raised by Nomane had already been addressed in earlier Supreme Court rulings, making the current application as without merit. 

The court found that the applicant was not raising any new significant constitutional issue and that the application had “no reasonable prospect of succeeding”.

Marape said the Supreme Court’s detailed judgment confirmed that constitutional amendment being questioned was validly enacted and consistent with the broader intent and structure of the Constitution.

Marape said PNG’s political and economic history clearly demonstrated the importance of stable governments.

“In the first 27 years of our nationhood, from 1975 to 2002, PNG changed governments 13 times. During that period, economic growth was slow,” he said.

“In the past 23 years, when the country had only three prime ministers, our economy experienced its fastest growth.

“These are historical facts tied directly to political stability and continuity in government.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Lawyers advised to correctly brief clients on law
item.description: JUSTICE Minister and Attorney-General Pila Niningi has urged lawyers to correctly brief their clients on the law. “Don’t push for something that’s not there, thinking there is a case. The five-man bench wasted time when we have other matters to look at. Correctly advise according to strength of the case,” he said.
item.content:encoded:
JUSTICE Minister and Attorney-General Pila Niningi has urged lawyers to correctly brief their clients on the law.

“Don’t push for something that’s not there, thinking there is a case. The five-man bench wasted time when we have other matters to look at. Correctly advise according to strength of the case,” he said. 

Niningi was responding to the Supreme Court decision dismissing a Section 18 (1) constitutional application filed by Opposition Leader James Nomane.

“If the lawyers do not advise correctly where the Supreme Court made the decision and they allow their clients and give false hope that there is something when there isn’t, there will be costs involved,” he said.

He thanked the Nomane for again testing the strength of Section 145(5) of the Constitution passed by Parliament in March last year.

“Section 145 (5) had been challenged several times. I welcome the Court’s decision,” he said.

“This law has stabilised the country and the vote of no confidence seems to be a thing of the past. If they (Opposition) want to move a vote of no confidence, then they must have the support and numbers.”

Niningi said the case filed in March followed a previous case in 2025 challenging section 145(5) brought by East Sepik Governor Allan Bird and Nomane, both of which were dismissed.

He said that there was another pending slip rule application which he had instructed lawyers to write to the Opposition to consider withdrawing the application.

“In the event that they continue, costs will be involved in dismissing cases in future applications,” he added.

He said the Supreme Court had again confirmed that Section 145(5) of the Constitution was valid.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Veupunama outlast Haidana challenge
item.description: DESPITE Haidana Lightning’s spirited efforts against Veupunama in their premier division clash on Saturday, Veupunama’s superior coordination and combination proved decisive in securing a 56-45 victory. The main game tipped off at 4pm at Rita Flynn, drawing a crowd of teams and supporters eager to witness the fierce contest.
item.content:encoded:
DESPITE Haidana Lightning’s spirited efforts against Veupunama in their premier division clash on Saturday, Veupunama’s superior coordination and combination proved decisive in securing a 56-45 victory.

The main game tipped off at 4pm at Rita Flynn, drawing a crowd of teams and supporters eager to witness the fierce contest.

With shooters Kala Ali and Joanne Okirua leading the attack, Veupunama established early dominance, capitalising on turnovers created by Tina Poha and Emariam Kepo, supported by centre Barbara Arivusu.

Haidana responded with the inclusion of the Andrew sisters – Rayleen at goal shoot and Waisame in centre – forming a strong attacking partnership.

The first quarter ended 18-8 in Veupunama’s favour and, by halftime, they had stretched the margin to 33-19.

Haidana lifted their intensity in the third quarter, with Rayleen finding her rhythm, narrowing the gap to 42-34.

The final quarter saw both sides push hard with equal speed and intensity, but Veupunama maintained control to finish 11 points clear, sealing the win at 56-45.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Seven heaven for Walker as Maroons unveil new blood
item.description: Brisbane: Sam Walker has been backed to guide Queensland from No.7 as Dolphins prop Thomas Flegler makes an inspirational return to the State of Origin arena. The Maroons have stuck solid with 11 players that won the Origin decider last year in the historic 24-12 triumph in Sydney, while naming six debutants in a 20-man squad to contest the Origin series opener, also in Sydney, on May 27.
item.content:encoded:
Brisbane: Sam Walker has been backed to guide Queensland from No.7 as Dolphins prop Thomas Flegler makes an inspirational return to the State of Origin arena.

The Maroons have stuck solid with 11 players that won the Origin decider last year in the historic 24-12 triumph in Sydney, while naming six debutants in a 20-man squad to contest the Origin series opener, also in Sydney, on May 27.

Sydney Roosters playmaker Walker joins Dolphins pocket rocket Max Plath (lock) and Gold Coast winger Jojo Fifita as the new players in the starting side. Cronulla forward Briton Nikora (No.14) and Brisbane No.6 Ezra Mam (No.18) are listed on the six-man bench. Dolphins powerhouse Kulikefu Finefeuiaki is 20th man.

The omission of Broncos and Australia fullback Reece Walsh, below his best in recent weeks, will be a surprise to some but Newcastle talisman Kalyn Ponga has a wonderful pedigree in Origin football at No.1.

Walker was likened this week by Darren Lockyer to a modern-day Allan Langer due to his unpredictability and brilliance.

The 23-year-old has proven to be a big-game player through 96 NRL games. The Maroons brains trust are convinced he is ready to unleash his best at halfback after last year’s No.7 and player of the Origin series Tom Dearden suffered a syndesmosis injury playing for North Queensland.

Flegler spent 677 days on the sidelines due to a shoulder nerve issue. His return to action this year was an inspirational story of dedication and commitment in itself.

To be backed by Slater to again lead the pack as a starting front-rower in Origin footy speaks volumes for the high esteem he is held in. The 26-year-old’s firebrand ways and uncompromising mentality up front will be crucial.

Fifita has been one of the Titans’ best this season after being switched from wing to centre. He plays both positions with aplomb and will partner recalled Dolphins winger Selwyn Cobbo whose last month of football has seen him at his most damaging best.

Plath has been given a great responsibility at starting lock and also gives Slater contingencies with his versatility.

His stellar display in the 32-10 win over South Sydney on Thursday night as a ball-playing lock after starting at hooker sealed his selection.

“If Billy gives me a call I would be so stoked,” Plath said earlier this month.

“My preferred position is playing lock and in the middle but you have to be fit to play hooker. Getting a lot of confidence there has added another string to my bow. I can play back-row as well.”

QUEENSLAND TEAM: 1. Kalyn Ponga, 2. Selwyn Cobbo, 3. Robert Toia, 4. Hamiso Tabuai-Fidow, 5. Jojo Fifita, 6. Cameron Munster, 7. Sam Walker, 8. Thomas Flegler, 9. Harry Grant, 10. Tino Fa’asuamaleaui, 11, Reuben Cotter, 12. Kurt Capewell, 13. Max Plath, 14. Briton Nikora, 15, Lindsay Collins, 16, Pat Carrigan, 17, Trent Loiero, 18. Ezra Mam, 19. Gehamat Shibasaki, 20. Kulikefu Finefeuiaki.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Tedesco Origin recall, NSW name six debutants
item.description: Sydney: James Tedesco’s State of Origin exile is over with the Sydney Roosters captain seeing off Dylan Edwards to reclaim his crown as NSW’s premier fullback.  Tedesco – the most-capped fullback in Blues history – has usurped Penrith marathon man Edwards to be named in the No.1 jersey for the May 27 series opener in Sydney.
item.content:encoded:
Sydney: James Tedesco’s State of Origin exile is over with the Sydney Roosters captain seeing off Dylan Edwards to reclaim his crown as NSW’s premier fullback. 

Tedesco – the most-capped fullback in Blues history – has usurped Penrith marathon man Edwards to be named in the No.1 jersey for the May 27 series opener in Sydney.

Picking between Edwards, who started all three games of last year’s losing series, was the hardest selection call Blues coach Laurie Daley had to make. 

But the form of the 33-year-old Tedesco, who has six tries and eight try assists across 10 games for the Roosters this season, proved too hard to resist.

Game one will mark Tedesco’s 24th Blues appearance and his first since the opening game of the 2024 series.

Tedesco has previously played with the majority of Daley’s NSW side, which contains six debutants including Manly flyer Tolu Koula, Roosters lock Victor Radley and Cronulla duo Addin Fonua-Blake and Blayke Brailey.

Fonua-Blake and Radley have previously represented New Zealand and England in Test football but a change in eligibility rules over the off-season has permitted both players to make their Origin bows. 

Koula, meanwhile, is named to line up on the wing alongside Brian To’o with Kotoni Staggs and Stephen Crichton picked in the centres.

The pair will be tasked with filling the void left by South Sydney’s Latrell Mitchell, who withdrew from selection due to the fact he has struggling with a back injury over recent weeks. 

Canberra’s Ethan Strange and Penrith’s Casey McLean are both uncapped and have been named on the bench alongside Radley, Cameron Murray and the recalled Jacob Saifiti. 

The rest of the NSW side is as expected with Nathan Cleary partnering Mitchell Moses in the halves and Reece Robson selected at hooker.

NSW SQUAD FOR GAME ONE: James Tedesco, Brian To’o, Stephen Crichton, Kotoni Staggs, Tolu Koula*, Mitchell Moses, Nathan Cleary, Addin Fonua-Blake, Reece Robson, Mitch Barnett, Hudson Young, Haumole Olakau’atu, Isaah Yeo (capt). Interchange: Cameron Murray, Victor Radley*, Jacob Saifiti, Blayke Brailey*, Ethan Strange*, Casey McLean*. Reserve: Dylan Lucas*.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Rough ride for Hunters. Tough task ahead on home soil
item.description: IT is a must-win QRL Hostplus Cup encounter for the SP PNG Hunters when they host South Logan Magpies at home at the Santos National Football Stadium, Port Moresby, on Sunday. The Hunters enter Round 10 sitting second last on the ladder with only two wins from eight games, while the Magpies remain one of the stronger teams in the competition.
item.content:encoded:
IT is a must-win QRL Hostplus Cup encounter for the SP PNG Hunters when they host South Logan Magpies at home at the Santos National Football Stadium, Port Moresby, on Sunday.

The Hunters enter Round 10 sitting second last on the ladder with only two wins from eight games, while the Magpies remain one of the stronger teams in the competition.

Despite the tough season, coach Paul Aiton believes his side is improving every week.

“We can see small improvements in every game,” Aiton said.

“Our defence has improved and our completion rate has improved too.”

The Hunters have struggled with injuries and suspensions all season, forcing major changes to the squad nearly every week.

This weekend, experienced forward Sylvester Namo is expected to add size and experience to the forward pack.

Seal Kalo also returns to the squad, while several regular players remain unavailable through injury and suspension.

Aiton said the team understands the importance of the match.

“We’ve been saying every game is a must-win, but we still believe we can make the top eight,” he said.

The coach also warned his players not to underestimate any team in the competition.

“Just because a team is lower on the ladder doesn’t mean you will beat them,” Aiton said.

With home crowd advantage, the Hunters could spring surprises that will keep their finals hopes alive.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Urgent need for logistics, leadership
item.description: PAPUA New Guinea approaches a critical democratic moment with General Election 2027 (GE27), yet concerns about preparations cannot be ignored. Reports from the recent Electoral Commission workshop in Waigani highlight incomplete preparations, including an outdated common roll, unresolved security arrangements, unfinished polling schedules, and administrative delays.
item.content:encoded:
PAPUA New Guinea approaches a critical democratic moment with General Election 2027 (GE27), yet concerns about preparations cannot be ignored.

Reports from the recent Electoral Commission workshop in Waigani highlight incomplete preparations, including an outdated common roll, unresolved security arrangements, unfinished polling schedules, and administrative delays.

The nation still recalls challenges during the GE22 – violence, delayed declarations, disputed outcomes, and public frustration – which weakened confidence in the electoral process.

To avoid a repeat, the Government and Electoral Commission must act urgently, ensuring transparency, proper planning, and decisive leadership.

Citizens need assurance that funding, logistics, staffing, and security are coordinated well ahead of polling.

Stable leadership, including the confirmation of acting electoral commissioner Margaret Vagi, is crucial for credibility.

Security forces must remain politically neutral, focusing solely on law and order.

Urgent action now is essential to safeguard democracy, strengthen national stability, and maintain public trust.

Preparation delayed is democracy denied.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Shadow over judicial bench
item.description: THE recent handling of allegations against Papua New Guinea’s chief justice by the Ombudsman Commission has generated uncertainty and concern about the transparency of institutional processes. Initially, the chief justice was referred to the Acting Public Prosecutor over allegations of misconduct and was subsequently cleared.
item.content:encoded:
THE recent handling of allegations against Papua New Guinea’s chief justice by the Ombudsman Commission has generated uncertainty and concern about the transparency of institutional processes.

Initially, the chief justice was referred to the Acting Public Prosecutor over allegations of misconduct and was subsequently cleared.

However, the Ombudsman Commission later referred the matter again, this time to the National Executive Council.

This sequence of events has left citizens questioning the clarity, consistency, and accountability of procedures affecting the judiciary.

Confidence in institutions is crucial for national stability and development, and prolonged uncertainty can undermine public trust, both domestically and internationally.

For PNG’s governance and judiciary to maintain credibility, it is essential that authorities provide clear communication, ensure transparency, and act decisively to resolve outstanding issues.

The people of PNG deserve clarity and assurance that justice is being served, and that institutional integrity remains protected.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Court challenge matters for democracy
item.description: IN a political environment where silence is often easier, Opposition Leader James Nomane’s decision to take his constitutional concerns to the Supreme Court demonstrates commitment to accountability and the rule of law. Although the Court dismissed his reference on Constitutional Amendment No.48, his willingness to challenge the system reflects the persistence and courage healthy democracies requi
item.content:encoded:
IN a political environment where silence is often easier, Opposition Leader James Nomane’s decision to take his constitutional concerns to the Supreme Court demonstrates commitment to accountability and the rule of law.

Although the Court dismissed his reference on Constitutional Amendment No.48, his willingness to challenge the system reflects the persistence and courage healthy democracies require.

Leadership is not measured only by victories, but by acting on principle when the stakes are high.

Nomane sought clarity on an issue affecting Parliament’s functions and motions of no confidence, ensuring it was formally examined rather than left to speculation.

Even without a favourable outcome, his action reinforces the role of the Opposition in scrutinising government, upholding constitutional limits, and keeping critical debates alive.

Political courage involves principle over convenience, and Nomane’s reference strengthens Papua New Guinea’s democratic institutions by showing that constitutional processes exist to hold power to account.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Equity must match accountability
item.description: PRIME Minister James Marape recently stressed the importance of accountability and the proper use of public funds. The fair distribution of District Services Improvement Programme (DSIP) and Provincial Services Improvement Programme (PSIP) funding to both Government and Opposition MPs reflects a positive democratic principle: national development should not depend on political loyalty.
item.content:encoded:
PRIME Minister James Marape recently stressed the importance of accountability and the proper use of public funds.

The fair distribution of District Services Improvement Programme (DSIP) and Provincial Services Improvement Programme (PSIP) funding to both Government and Opposition MPs reflects a positive democratic principle: national development should not depend on political loyalty.

Every district and province deserves equal opportunity for progress.

However, equitable funding alone is insufficient.

Many Papua New Guineans continue to face deteriorating roads, limited health services, overcrowded classrooms, and restricted economic opportunities, despite years of DSIP and PSIP allocations.

Citizens now expect more than speeches or ceremonial launches – they demand transparency, value for money, and tangible results.

All MPs should publicly disclose annual expenditure reports, project contracts, timelines, and audit outcomes.

Stronger oversight by provincial administrations, local communities, civil society, and churches is essential. 

The future of Papua New Guinea depends on responsibly managing public funds, making accountability and service delivery the true measure of political leadership.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Neidab dedicates degree, award to late father
item.description: WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father. “I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”
item.content:encoded:
WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father.

“I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”

Neidab lost his father Ulu Rusini last year during the second semester of his final year – a critical time for any final year university student.

However, he managed to pull through and graduated with a Bachelor in Public Finance and Accountancy degree.

Neidab, 39, from Wanigela Village, Abau District, Central, is the second eldest of six children.

He was among those who graduated from the University of PNG School of Business and Public Policy last Thursday.

To his employer, the Public Service Commission, he was an investment which paid off when he not only graduated but also named the top performing student in the Bachelor of Public Finance and Accountancy class.

He received the Academic Excellence Award after being sponsored by the PSC, where he has worked for 11 years, to pursue the degree while working as the acting Senior Budget Officer and Certifying Officer.

It was an opportunity he took seriously.

“I thank my employer, the Public Service Commission, for the investment and opportunity and trust placed in me to send me for further studies.”

His work involves budget support, expenditure management, and financial advisory duties. He knows that the degree will help him in his work and lift his performance.

“What motivated me to go back for further studies was to enhance my skills in my current duties on the processes in place to manage the public funds effectively and efficiently.”

Neidab attained a Certificate in Business Studies in 2007 and a Diploma in Business Studies in 2008 at the Port Moresby Business College.

In 2023, he graduated with a Diploma in Accounting from the Somare Institute of Leadership and Governance (SILAG).

The loss of his father affected him. On top of that, he faced financial issues and tried his best to juggle his time between work, study, and family.

“I encountered financial challenges attending classes, attending to family matters, as well as work.”

At times when work and study demanded his attention most, he prioritised which one to tackle first.

“I’ve had clashes between my studies and my job. Some time, I had to attend to my work first and then my studies second because work is my bread and butter.”

The experience changed how he managed his time.

“It shaped me as a person to think critically and handle issues in a way that is balanced.”

At home, his wife Wendy Ulu and two sons Neidrolson who is in Grade Six, and Braden in Grade 4, endured nights when he had to burn the midnight oil.

Wife Wendy remembers those nights when they hardly went to sleep as Neidab would spend hours studying and keeping the lights on.

So she was happy and relieved to see him graduate with flying colours – a fitting reward for his hard work and perseverance. For the family, the hours of struggling to sleep are finally over.

Neidab dedicated the award to his employer, his late father, and his family.

“I dedicate the award to my employer and to my father who should have attended and witnessed this ceremony. And not forgetting my family who supported me throughout this journey.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG’s frontier judges: peace-building from the bench
item.description: IN Papua New Guinea’s most conflict-torn provinces, an unlikely group of actors is stepping into the peacebuilding breach: resident National Court judges. Armed with an unusually expansive constitutional mandate, some of these judges are deploying their powers not just to process the aftermath of violence, but to try to prevent it.
item.content:encoded:
IN Papua New Guinea’s most conflict-torn provinces, an unlikely group of actors is stepping into the peacebuilding breach: resident National Court judges.

Armed with an unusually expansive constitutional mandate, some of these judges are deploying their powers not just to process the aftermath of violence, but to try to prevent it.

The scale of the challenge is hard to overstate. In Hela and Enga – two of the Highlands provinces hardest hit by inter-group conflict – sophisticated weaponry, warlord networks, and overwhelmed police have combined to produce a crisis that conventional state responses have struggled to contain.

Mobile squad deployments, special policing operations and draconian legislative announcements are all being experimented with.

But quietly, on the ground, judges are trying something different.

As reported in one of the daily newspapers on Feb 19, Justice Andrew Kostopolous, the resident National Court Judge in Hela, used a court-sanctioned inquiry into tribal fighting and lawlessness to confront the province’s deepening governance crisis head-on.

In proceedings marked by unusual candour, he issued bans on alcohol and the public carrying of bush knives, ordered the surrender of firearms, and named specific warlords as criminals subject to immediate arrest.

Justice Kostopolous also issued a human rights violation order against opposing factions in a tribal fight near Tari town, and delivered a stern warning that the province was “teetering on the edge of a constitutional crisis”.

“If the police aren’t doing their job, that’s a constitutional crisis,” he said.

“If the executive is not doing its job, that’s a constitutional crisis.”

These provisions transform PNG judges from passive arbiters into “social engineers”.

The Supreme Court has affirmed that the Constitution is to be interpreted broadly, as “goal-oriented, purposive” and “liberal”, not in a “strict, technical and legalistic” manner.

Judges are urged to “think expansively and to be dynamic” and to “use judicial ingenuity to do justice”.

This is an unusual mandate, though not a unique one: judges in South Africa and India operate under similarly interventionist frameworks.

Three constitutional provisions in PNG are central to this logic.

Section 57 empowers the Supreme and National Courts to protect guaranteed rights and freedoms – either on application or on their own initiative.

Under this provision judges have summoned police to investigate alleged rights breaches, order the closure of prisons on humanitarian grounds, and directed law enforcement commissioners on the management of their facilities.

Critically, Section 57(5) extends this power to cases of “actual or imminent infringement”, allowing courts to make anticipatory orders before a rights breach has fully materialised.

Section 22 grants courts the power to enforce constitutional duties and to formulate procedures and mechanisms where existing frameworks are insufficient.

Section 225 obliges state agencies and service providers to actively support constitutional bodies, including the courts themselves.

Taken together, these provisions transform PNG judges from passive arbiters into what legal scholar Bal Kama describes as “social engineers” – actors empowered to identify systemic failures and act to address them.

In Enga, resident judge Justice Graham Ellis has been equally active.

 Last month, he issued a series of orders on his own initiative aimed at stopping tribal fighting in the Laiagam area.

These included a ban on carrying machetes and axes in public, the establishment of police roadblocks in the Mulitaka area, and orders for police to arrest anyone breaching National Court directives.

He explicitly framed these interventions as a matter of human rights enforcement under Section 57 of the Constitution, noting that the fighting affected rights to life, freedom, protection of the law, freedom of assembly, and freedom of movement.

Justice Ellis’s reasoning is worth quoting at length, because it illuminates the distinctive logic that underpins these interventions: “In many parts of the world, judges can do no more than consider the effects of criminal conduct, notably conducting trials after it is alleged that a crime has been committed. However, the inclusion of human rights provisions in the Constitution of PNG enables a judge to take a more active role by making orders, where it is considered appropriate, that can try to stop and even prevent criminal conduct.”

Both judges are thinking systemically, not just case by case.

Rather than waiting for individual matters to come before them and then ruling on them in isolation, they are using the bench as a vantage point from which to map the conditions generating violence – and to issue orders aimed at disrupting them.

This is a direct challenge to what one judge has described to us as the conventional posture of the justice system: “We tend to come in after everything has happened.

“We wait until it has happened and then clean up the mess.”

item.title: Liberty Liko becomes fourth doctor in family
item.description: HER parents are doctors. Her brother is one too. And so is her cousin. So when it came to choosing a career, Liberty Liko needed little persuasion on what she wanted to be. “Medicine is all I have ever known. And since I was a little girl, I dreamt of becoming a doctor.”
item.content:encoded:
HER parents are doctors. Her brother is one too. And so is her cousin. So when it came to choosing a career, Liberty Liko needed little persuasion on what she wanted to be.

“Medicine is all I have ever known. And since I was a little girl, I dreamt of becoming a doctor.”

Her father is Dr Osbourne Liko, a surgeon and urologist, who also served as Health Secretary and Medical Board chairman.

Her mother Dr. Mary-Julian Baki is a pediatrician who had served in Gulf, Chimbu and Port Moresby for a total of 23 years. She is now in private practice.

Her brother Dr Malachi Liko graduated in 2020 with a Bachelor in Medicine, Bachelor in Surgery (MBBS) degree. He currently works as a general practitioner.

Her first cousin on her father’s side, Dr. Joyce Sonoling, is a psychiatrist.

On top of that, her family’s close friends include Dr Mathias Sapuri and his daughter Dr Yvonne Sapuri.

Liberty, 23, was among 400-plus students who graduated from the University of Papua New Guinea School of Medicine and Health Science last week.

She had her face painted in Kairuku to honor her Mum, who is the first from Waima in Kairuku to become a doctor.

Liberty wore a Morobe bilum and necklace to honor her Morobe heritage and father who is from Pindu in Finschaffen.

She dressed in the same way as her mother when she graduated from UPNG in 1992. It was to honour her and other women choosing the profession.

“Now that my dream is a reality, I am overwhelmed with gratitude, first and foremost, to our ever merciful God, and to my backbone, my parents.

“I am equally thankful to my entire support network as this achievement is just as much theirs as it is mine. I’ve never had to look far for good role models and I aspire to be even half the doctor my parents are.”

Her parents supported her education into university. She also secured a Government scholarship.

She attended primary school in Australia because of her mother’s overseas work attachment. She later attended the Port Moresby Grammar School.

She completed grades seven to 12 at the St Joseph International College in Port Moresby in 2019 before she was accepted into UPNG on an academic excellence scholarship in 2020. But Covid-19 affected her studies. So to get into medical school, she dropped the Science degree and withdrew from UPNG.

She enrolled at the Pacific Adventist University in 2021 for the Science Foundation with the aim of getting into the School of Medicine and Health Sciences. She attended the UPNG medical school from 2022 to 2025.

She will decide what branch of medicine she is to pursue once she completes her residency and internship.

Dad Dr Liko is proud of her achievement.

“Liberty earned her achievements on her own merits unequivocally. In fact, she had an enormous shoe to fill throughout her journey in pursuit of her medical career. Nearly every lecturer knew her, a majority of doctors and nurses knew who she was.

“But she has always been herself – level-headed, cool, calm and collective. She respects everybody despite any opinion they may have on her. She is always considerate of her less fortunate school mates whose parents are not in Port Moresby.

“She earned her MBBS through disciplined study, always have resilient study habits, perseverance and consistent hard work over five demanding years.

“Despite her family’s medical backgrounds and public roles, I’m proud to say that she never sought advantage or special treatment, choosing instead to uphold fairness and independence.”

Mum Dr Baki described Liberty as confident, determined and an intelligent young lady who always strives for perfection.

“She was no dainty girl growing up and always fought with her six older siblings to be heard. I am very proud of her as a mother and a female professional.”

But she knows well what lies ahead of Liberty.

“This is just the beginning of a long journey of sleepless nights, no weekends or public holidays, missing out on family events and sacrifice of personal time. It is a calling to serve.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.
